does: This paper proposes Alternate Preference Optimization (AltPO), a method for machine unlearning in large language models that eliminates knowledge of specific data while maintaining model utility. Unlike prior unlearning approaches that rely only on negative feedback to suppress forget-set responses, AltPO combines negative feedback with in-domain positive feedback to guide the model toward sensible, consistent outputs about the forget set. The authors introduce new evaluation metrics to measure response quality for forgotten information and demonstrate through experiments that their approach achieves effective unlearning without causing nonsensical outputs, privacy violations, or degradation in overall model performance.
connects: This work builds on the machine unlearning literature, which seeks efficient removal of training data influence from deployed models following GDPR and privacy regulations. It extends prior LLM unlearning methods that primarily used negative feedback signals, which led to problematic outputs like refusals or degraded reasoning. The paper connects to preference-based optimization frameworks such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), adapting these techniques to the unlearning setting by constructing paired comparisons between undesired forget-set responses and sensible alternatives. It also responds to known weaknesses in existing unlearning approaches where purely suppressive feedback causes model brittleness and utility loss.
enables: This work enables more practical deployment of unlearning in commercial LLMs where maintaining user experience and model reliability during knowledge removal is critical. It sets up future research on combining privacy objectives with utility preservation in model adaptation, potentially extending to other model editing and fine-tuning scenarios. The introduction of quality metrics for forgotten knowledge opens avenues for better evaluation frameworks in unlearning research. The approach could be extended to unlearning harmful behaviors or copyrighted content while keeping models functional, and may inspire hybrid feedback mechanisms in other safety-critical model modification tasks where both suppression and positive guidance are needed.
